summary: Uncle Bob argues that developers need to fundamentally shift their belief about TDD from thinking it costs time to believing it always saves time. He critiques an article that suggests unit testing should be 'actively justified' before use, arguing this effectively means no one will write tests. The post presents TDD as a non-negotiable discipline that speeds up development in every situation without exception. Martin's central claim is that once you internalize this belief through practice, you'll never abandon TDD even under extreme pressure.
key_points: The belief that unit tests take time is a mental switch ('THE BIT') that needs to be flipped
Requiring developers to 'actively justify' unit testing effectively discourages all testing
TDD saves time in every case - not just long-term, but even for the shortest tasks
The distinction between TDD and tests-after-the-fact is crucial; TDD is a discipline, not just a testing practice
The way to flip the bit is simply to practice TDD consistently for about a month
money_quotes: I want you to believe that Test Driven Development saves time in every case and every situation without exception amen.
THE BIT is the boolean variable within your subconscious that represents your belief that unit tests take time.
The more the pressure is on, the more you'll rely on the TDD discipline.
I might not use TDD if I needed the task to take a really long time to finish, and have lots of bugs. But other than that? No, no case at all.
Who'd be fool enough to take that risk?
It seems to me that you can lose a lot of money by shipping code that doesn't work too, but – oh well.
key_insight: TDD is not a time cost to be justified but a time-saving discipline to be internalized as an unshakeable belief through consistent practice.
